title: AnimationPlayer, Tween & AnimationTree State Machines
html:
From a moving box to a living character

In the last lesson you built a side-scrolling platformer with CharacterBody2D, gravity, jumping, a TileMapLayer level, and a follow camera. It works, but it feels mechanical: the player slides around as a static sprite. This lesson is about game feel — the layer of animation and motion that makes a character feel alive. You will learn three distinct animation tools and exactly when each one earns its place.

ToolUse it for
AnimationPlayerAuthored, keyframed animations on any property (frames, position, modulate)
TweenShort, code-driven, one-off motions (squash on land, flash on hit)
AnimationTreeBlending and switching between many animations via a state machine

Sprite frames vs AnimationPlayer

For simple frame flipping, an AnimatedSprite2D with a SpriteFrames resource is enough. But the moment you want to animate more than frames — say, scale and frames together — you want AnimationPlayer, which can keyframe any property of any node in the scene. Add an AnimationPlayer node as a child of the player. In its bottom panel, create animations named idle, run, jump, and fall. For each, keyframe the frame property of your Sprite2D (or use a sprite sheet via Region).

Coyote time and jump buffering

Two tiny timers separate a frustrating platformer from a great one. Coyote time lets the player jump for a few frames after walking off a ledge. Jump buffering remembers a jump press made slightly before landing.

player.gd
# extends CharacterBody2D const SPEED := 200.0 const JUMP_VELOCITY := -420.0 const COYOTE := 0.1 const BUFFER := 0.12 var _coyote := 0.0 var _buffer := 0.0 @onready var sprite: Sprite2D = $Sprite2D @onready var anim: AnimationPlayer = $AnimationPlayer func _physics_process(delta: float) -> void: velocity += get_gravity() * delta # tick the timers down _coyote = (COYOTE if is_on_floor() else _coyote - delta) _buffer -= delta if Input.is_action_just_pressed("jump"): _buffer = BUFFER if _buffer > 0.0 and _coyote > 0.0: velocity.y = JUMP_VELOCITY _buffer = 0.0 _coyote = 0.0 var dir := Input.get_axis("left", "right") velocity.x = dir * SPEED if dir != 0.0: sprite.flip_h = dir < 0.0 move_and_slide() _update_anim(dir)

Driving animations from state

Rather than scatter anim.play() calls everywhere, derive the animation from the body's current state once per frame:

player.gd (cont.)
func _update_anim(dir: float) -> void: if not is_on_floor(): anim.play("jump" if velocity.y < 0.0 else "fall") elif absf(dir) > 0.01: anim.play("run") else: anim.play("idle")
Tip.
Calling anim.play("run") when run is already playing is a no-op — Godot will not restart it. So calling it every frame is safe and cheap.

Squash and stretch with Tween

A Tween is a throwaway object you create in code to animate values over time. It is perfect for reactive juice. Detect the landing frame and pop a squash:

player.gd (land juice)
var _was_in_air := false func _check_landing() -> void: if _was_in_air and is_on_floor(): var t := create_tween() sprite.scale = Vector2(1.3, 0.7) t.tween_property(sprite, "scale", Vector2.ONE, 0.18) \ .set_trans(Tween.TRANS_ELASTIC).set_ease(Tween.EASE_OUT) _was_in_air = not is_on_floor()

Call _check_landing() at the end of _physics_process. The body squashes flat on impact, then springs back with an elastic ease. This one effect does more for feel than any amount of sprite art.

When to graduate to AnimationTree

For a platformer with four states the _update_anim approach is fine. But as you add crouch, wall-slide, attack, and hurt, the if/elif ladder rots. AnimationTree with an AnimationNodeStateMachine lets you author states and transitions visually, with conditions and blend times. Add an AnimationTree, set its anim_player to your AnimationPlayer, choose StateMachine as the root, and drag in your animations as states. In code you travel between them:

state machine travel
@onready var sm: AnimationNodeStateMachinePlayback = \ $AnimationTree.get("parameters/playback") # later, instead of anim.play("run"): sm.travel("run")
Lab.
Add a BlendSpace1D inside the tree blending idle and run by horizontal speed, so the run animation speeds up smoothly with velocity instead of snapping on.
🎮 Build This: Platformer — Juicy Hero

This extends the side-scrolling platformer from Lesson 8 — same project, same level, now with animation and feel.

  1. Add an AnimationPlayer with idle, run, jump, fall animations keyframing your sprite frames.
  2. Wire _update_anim() so animation follows velocity and floor state every frame.
  3. Add coyote time and jump buffering with the two timers shown.
  4. Add the landing squash-and-stretch Tween, plus a quick stretch when jumping.
  5. Convert your animation logic to an AnimationTree state machine and add one new state (crouch or wall-slide) to prove the structure scales.

title: Metroidvania — Connected Rooms, Abilities & Persistence
html:
One level becomes a world

A metroidvania is a platformer whose map is the puzzle. The player explores connected rooms, hits a locked door or an unreachable ledge, finds an ability elsewhere, and comes back to open the way. To build one on top of your juicy platformer you need three new systems: room-to-room scene transitions, ability gates, and persistent world state so a door you opened stays open after you leave the room.

Each room is a scene

Make every room its own .tscn (a Node2D root with a TileMapLayer and entities). The player should not live inside a room scene — that way it survives room changes. Instead, keep the player and the camera in a persistent layer managed by a World autoload. Recall from Lesson 5 that an autoload is a singleton node loaded before any scene.

world.gd (autoload)
# extends Node — registered as autoload "World" var abilities: Dictionary = {} # "double_jump": true var flags: Dictionary = {} # "door_a_open": true var current_room: Node = null var spawn_tag: String = "default" func has_ability(id: String) -> bool: return abilities.get(id, false) func grant(id: String) -> void: abilities[id] = true func set_flag(id: String) -> void: flags[id] = true func has_flag(id: String) -> bool: return flags.get(id, false)

The transition Area2D

Place an Area2D doorway at each room edge. It stores the path of the room to load and a spawn tag telling the next room where to drop the player. When the player overlaps, it asks the World to swap rooms.

door.gd
# extends Area2D @export_file("*.tscn") var target_room: String @export var target_spawn: String = "default" func _ready() -> void: body_entered.connect(_on_body_entered) func _on_body_entered(body: Node) -> void: if body.is_in_group("player"): World.spawn_tag = target_spawn World.change_room(target_room)

Swapping rooms without losing the player

The World frees the old room, instances the new one, finds the matching spawn marker, and teleports the persistent player there. Use call_deferred so you never free a node mid-collision (a classic crash).

world.gd (room swap)
@onready var room_holder: Node = get_tree().root.get_node("Main/Rooms") @onready var player: Node2D = get_tree().root.get_node("Main/Player") func change_room(path: String) -> void: _do_change.call_deferred(path) func _do_change(path: String) -> void: if current_room: current_room.queue_free() var scene := load(path) as PackedScene current_room = scene.instantiate() room_holder.add_child(current_room) var marker := current_room.find_child("Spawn_" + spawn_tag, true, false) if marker: player.global_position = marker.global_position current_room.apply_flags() # let the room hide opened doors etc.

Ability gates

A gate is just a check against the World. A high ledge needs a double jump; a barrier needs a dash. The player reads abilities each frame:

player.gd (gated double jump)
var _jumps := 0 func _try_jump() -> void: var max_jumps := 2 if World.has_ability("double_jump") else 1 if is_on_floor(): _jumps = 0 if Input.is_action_just_pressed("jump") and _jumps < max_jumps: velocity.y = JUMP_VELOCITY _jumps += 1

An ability pickup is an Area2D that calls World.grant("double_jump"), sets a flag so it never respawns, then frees itself. Because the flag lives in the autoload, re-entering the room keeps it gone.

Persistence and saving

Because all meaningful state is in two dictionaries, saving the game is trivial — serialize them to JSON or a Godot resource. When a room loads, apply_flags() walks its objects and asks the World whether each should be open/collected:

room.gd
func apply_flags() -> void: for d in get_tree().get_nodes_in_group("gate"): if World.has_flag(d.flag_id): d.queue_free()
Watch out.
Never queue_free() a room from inside that room's own Area2D signal — defer it. Freeing the node whose physics callback you are inside will crash or corrupt the physics step.
Law.
Persistent game state belongs in an autoload, never in a scene that gets freed. Scenes are disposable; the singleton is the source of truth.
🎮 Build This: Metroidvania — Three Connected Caverns

This extends your platformer/juicy hero into a metroidvania — reuse the player, animations, and tiles; add the world structure.

  1. Split your level into three room scenes joined by Door areas with target room + spawn tag.
  2. Add the World autoload holding abilities and flags; move the player out of rooms into a persistent Main/Player.
  3. Implement deferred change_room() that re-positions the player at Spawn_<tag>.
  4. Add a double-jump pickup that gates access to a ledge in another room.
  5. Make doors and pickups persist via World flags so backtracking shows your progress.

title: Top-Down Adventure — 8-Way Movement, Combat & a Zelda-like World
html:
A new genre: drop the gravity

This lesson starts a new genre — the top-down action-adventure, in the spirit of classic Zelda. The biggest mental shift from the platformer is that there is no gravity and no floor. The player moves freely on a 2D plane in eight directions, and the y-axis is depth on the map rather than up/down. You still use CharacterBody2D, but movement becomes a normalized direction vector.

Eight-way movement

player.gd
# extends CharacterBody2D const SPEED := 140.0 var facing := Vector2.DOWN func _physics_process(_delta: float) -> void: var dir := Input.get_vector("left", "right", "up", "down") velocity = dir * SPEED if dir != Vector2.ZERO: facing = dir # remember last move dir for attacks move_and_slide() _update_anim(dir)

Input.get_vector returns an already-normalized vector, so diagonal movement is not faster than cardinal — a bug you would otherwise have to fix by hand. We store facing so that when the player attacks while standing still, the sword still swings the right way.

Directional animation with blend positions

Top-down characters need idle and walk animations for four (or eight) directions. The cleanest approach is an AnimationTree with BlendSpace2D nodes — one for idle, one for walk — driven by the facing vector. Set the tree's blend parameters from code:

player.gd (anim)
@onready var tree: AnimationTree = $AnimationTree @onready var sm: AnimationNodeStateMachinePlayback = \ tree.get("parameters/playback") func _update_anim(dir: Vector2) -> void: tree.set("parameters/Idle/blend_position", facing) tree.set("parameters/Walk/blend_position", facing) sm.travel("Walk" if dir != Vector2.ZERO else "Idle")

Directional melee combat

A sword swing is a short-lived hitbox: an Area2D that is enabled for a few frames, positioned in the facing direction, and on its hurt layer. Enemies carry a matching hurtbox. This hitbox/hurtbox pattern (built on the collision layers/masks from the Asteroids lesson) is the backbone of all action combat.

player.gd (attack)
@onready var hitbox: Area2D = $Hitbox @onready var hb_shape: CollisionShape2D = $Hitbox/Shape var _attacking := false func _unhandled_input(e: InputEvent) -> void: if e.is_action_pressed("attack") and not _attacking: _attack() func _attack() -> void: _attacking = true hitbox.position = facing * 18.0 hb_shape.disabled = false await get_tree().create_timer(0.15).timeout hb_shape.disabled = true _attacking = false func _ready() -> void: hitbox.area_entered.connect(_on_hit) func _on_hit(area: Area2D) -> void: if area.has_method("take_damage"): area.take_damage(1)
Tip.
await get_tree().create_timer(t).timeout is GDScript's clean way to wait without a separate Timer node. The function pauses and resumes after the delay — perfect for short attack windows.

Interactables and pickups

An interactable (chest, sign, lever) is an Area2D with an interact() method. The player keeps a list of nearby interactables and acts on the closest when you press the action key:

chest.gd
# extends Area2D (group: "interactable") @export var item: String = "key" var _opened := false func interact() -> void: if _opened: return _opened = true Inventory.add(item) $Sprite2D.frame = 1 # open chest frame

A small Inventory autoload holds counts in a dictionary and emits a changed signal so the HUD updates itself — the decoupled, signal-driven pattern you learned in Breakout.

Room-based dungeon

Reuse the room-transition idea from the metroidvania, but top-down: each dungeon room is a scene, and doorway Area2D nodes swap rooms. A locked door checks Inventory.has("key") before opening — your first content gate built from combat plus inventory.

Lab.
Give enemies a hurtbox and a tiny take_damage() with a knockback impulse: velocity = (global_position - source).normalized() * 200 applied for a few frames. Knockback transforms hits from invisible to satisfying.
🎮 Build This: Top-Down Adventure — Dungeon Crawl

This starts the top-down adventure genre as a fresh project, reusing patterns (rooms, signals, hitbox/hurtbox) from earlier games.

  1. Build an 8-way CharacterBody2D player using Input.get_vector, tracking a facing vector.
  2. Drive 4-direction idle/walk via an AnimationTree with BlendSpace2D nodes.
  3. Add a directional sword Hitbox area and an enemy with a hurtbox and knockback.
  4. Create an Inventory autoload, a chest interactable, and a HUD that updates from its signal.
  5. Connect three dungeon rooms with door areas, one locked behind a key from a chest.

title: Twin-Stick Shooter — Aim, Bullet Patterns & Waves
html:
A new genre: aim independent of movement

This lesson starts the twin-stick shooter genre in a new project, though it reuses the top-down movement you just built. The defining mechanic is that you move with one input and aim with another — keyboard moves, mouse aims (or two analog sticks on a gamepad). On top of that you will learn object pooling for bullets and a wave manager that ramps difficulty.

Mouse aim

The player rotates a turret toward the mouse and fires along it. Aiming is just pointing at the global mouse position:

player.gd
# extends CharacterBody2D const SPEED := 220.0 @onready var muzzle: Marker2D = $Turret/Muzzle @onready var turret: Node2D = $Turret var _cooldown := 0.0 func _physics_process(delta: float) -> void: var dir := Input.get_vector("left", "right", "up", "down") velocity = dir * SPEED move_and_slide() turret.look_at(get_global_mouse_position()) _cooldown -= delta if Input.is_action_pressed("fire") and _cooldown <= 0.0: _fire() _cooldown = 0.12

Why pool bullets

A twin-stick game spawns hundreds of bullets a second. Calling instantiate() and queue_free() that often thrashes memory and stutters. A pool pre-creates a fixed set of bullets, hides them, and recycles them — you never allocate during gameplay. This is one of the most important optimization patterns in action games.

bullet_pool.gd (autoload)
# extends Node @export var bullet_scene: PackedScene var _pool: Array = [] const SIZE := 256 func _ready() -> void: for i in SIZE: var b := bullet_scene.instantiate() b.set_process(false) b.hide() add_child(b) _pool.append(b) func spawn(pos: Vector2, dir: Vector2) -> void: for b in _pool: if not b.visible: b.activate(pos, dir) return

The bullet itself deactivates instead of freeing when it expires or hits something:

bullet.gd
# extends Area2D var _vel := Vector2.ZERO var _life := 0.0 func activate(pos: Vector2, dir: Vector2) -> void: global_position = pos _vel = dir.normalized() * 600.0 _life = 1.5 show() set_process(true) func _process(delta: float) -> void: global_position += _vel * delta _life -= delta if _life <= 0.0: _deactivate() func _deactivate() -> void: hide() set_process(false)

Firing along the turret

player.gd (fire)
func _fire() -> void: var dir := Vector2.RIGHT.rotated(turret.rotation) BulletPool.spawn(muzzle.global_position, dir)

Bullet patterns

Enemies become interesting through patterns. A spread fires N bullets fanned around the aim; a ring fires evenly in a circle. Both are loops over angles:

enemy.gd (patterns)
func spread(center: Vector2, count: int, arc_deg: float) -> void: var step := deg_to_rad(arc_deg) / maxi(count - 1, 1) var start := center.angle() - deg_to_rad(arc_deg) / 2.0 for i in count: var a := start + step * i BulletPool.spawn(global_position, Vector2.RIGHT.rotated(a)) func ring(count: int) -> void: for i in count: var a := TAU * i / count BulletPool.spawn(global_position, Vector2.RIGHT.rotated(a))

The wave manager

An autoload (or scene node) spawns groups of enemies, waits until they are cleared, then spawns a harder wave. Track living enemies with a group:

waves.gd
@export var enemy_scene: PackedScene var wave := 0 func start() -> void: _next_wave() func _next_wave() -> void: wave += 1 var n := 3 + wave * 2 for i in n: var e := enemy_scene.instantiate() e.global_position = _ring_point(i, n) e.add_to_group("enemies") e.died.connect(_on_enemy_died) add_child(e) func _on_enemy_died() -> void: if get_tree().get_node_count_in_group("enemies") <= 1: await get_tree().create_timer(2.0).timeout _next_wave()

The upgrade loop

Between waves, offer a choice: faster fire rate, double shot, more speed. Store stats on the player and let an upgrade mutate them. This risk/reward cadence — survive, choose, grow stronger — is what makes the genre addictive.

Law.
If a game spawns and destroys the same kind of object constantly, pool it. Allocation during gameplay is the most common cause of frame-time spikes in action games.
Tip.
Put bullets on their own collision layer and let only enemies/walls mask it. Bullets should never collide with each other or the firing player.
🎮 Build This: Twin-Stick Shooter — Arena Survivor

This starts the twin-stick shooter genre in a new project, reusing top-down movement and the hitbox pattern from the adventure game.

  1. Build a player that moves with WASD and aims the turret at the mouse, firing on hold.
  2. Implement a BulletPool autoload that recycles 256 pre-instanced bullets.
  3. Add enemies that chase and fire spread and ring patterns.
  4. Add a Waves manager that scales enemy count each cleared wave.
  5. Show three upgrade cards between waves that permanently boost the player.

title: Into 3D — Cameras, Lighting & a First-Person Shooter
html:
The same engine, one more axis

This lesson starts the first-person shooter genre as a real 3D game. Everything you know transfers: nodes, scenes, signals, autoloads, the node lifecycle. The 3D nodes are the 2D ones with a 3D suffix and a z-axis. Node2D becomes Node3D; CharacterBody2D becomes CharacterBody3D; Vector2 becomes Vector3. The editor gains an orbit camera you fly with right-mouse + WASD.

Building a scene you can see

A 3D scene shows nothing until you add a camera and a light. Add a Node3D root, then:

  • WorldEnvironment with an Environment resource — set background to a sky and enable ambient light so nothing is pure black.
  • DirectionalLight3D — your sun; enable shadow_enabled.
  • MeshInstance3D with a BoxMesh scaled flat as the floor, and a StaticBody3D + CollisionShape3D so things stand on it.

Give meshes a StandardMaterial3D in their material_override slot to set color and roughness. That is the entire 3D rendering pipeline you need to start.

The first-person controller

An FPS body is a CharacterBody3D with a capsule collider and a Camera3D child at eye height. Mouse motion rotates the body (yaw) and the camera (pitch) separately, so looking up never tilts your movement.

fps_player.gd
# extends CharacterBody3D const SPEED := 5.0 const JUMP := 4.5 const SENS := 0.0025 @onready var cam: Camera3D = $Camera3D func _ready() -> void: Input.mouse_mode = Input.MOUSE_MODE_CAPTURED func _unhandled_input(e: InputEvent) -> void: if e is InputEventMouseMotion: rotate_y(-e.relative.x * SENS) # yaw on the body cam.rotate_x(-e.relative.y * SENS) # pitch on the camera cam.rotation.x = clampf(cam.rotation.x, -1.4, 1.4) func _physics_process(delta: float) -> void: if not is_on_floor(): velocity += get_gravity() * delta if Input.is_action_just_pressed("jump") and is_on_floor(): velocity.y = JUMP var input := Input.get_vector("left", "right", "up", "down") var dir := (transform.basis * Vector3(input.x, 0, input.y)).normalized() velocity.x = dir.x * SPEED velocity.z = dir.z * SPEED move_and_slide()
Tip.
transform.basis * Vector3(x,0,z) turns input that is relative to where you face into world-space movement. That single multiply is why W always goes forward no matter which way you turned.

Shooting with a raycast

Hitscan weapons fire instantly along a ray. Add a RayCast3D child of the camera pointing forward (target_position = Vector3(0,0,-100)). On fire, check what it hit:

fps_player.gd (shoot)
@onready var ray: RayCast3D = $Camera3D/RayCast3D func _shoot() -> void: ray.force_raycast_update() if ray.is_colliding(): var hit := ray.get_collider() if hit.has_method("take_damage"): hit.take_damage(25) _spawn_impact(ray.get_collision_point(), ray.get_collision_normal())

Call _shoot() from _unhandled_input on the fire action. force_raycast_update() makes the ray query the world this instant rather than waiting for the next physics frame, so the shot lands exactly when you click.

Enemies

An enemy is a CharacterBody3D that moves toward the player and has health. Basic homing uses the direction to the player:

enemy.gd
# extends CharacterBody3D var health := 100 const SPEED := 3.0 @onready var player := get_tree().get_first_node_in_group("player") func _physics_process(delta: float) -> void: if not is_on_floor(): velocity += get_gravity() * delta if player: var to := (player.global_position - global_position) to.y = 0 velocity.x = to.normalized().x * SPEED velocity.z = to.normalized().z * SPEED move_and_slide() func take_damage(amount: int) -> void: health -= amount if health <= 0: queue_free()

Mouse capture and pausing

While playing you capture the mouse; on pause or death, release it with Input.MOUSE_MODE_VISIBLE so the player can click UI. A ui_cancel toggle between the two is standard.

Watch out.
In 3D, forward is -Z, not +Z. A camera looks down its own -Z axis. If your character runs backward, you flipped a sign — check the input.y term and the ray's target.
Lab.
Add a muzzle flash (OmniLight3D flashed on for one frame) and a recoil kick that nudges cam.rotation.x up, then tweens it back. Instant FPS feel.
🎮 Build This: First-Person Shooter (3D) — Arena FPS

This starts the first-person shooter genre as a real 3D game in a new project.

  1. Build a 3D arena: floor + walls with collision, a DirectionalLight3D, and a WorldEnvironment sky.
  2. Make a CharacterBody3D FPS controller with captured-mouse look (yaw on body, pitch on camera) and jumping.
  3. Add a forward RayCast3D hitscan weapon with impact decals and damage.
  4. Spawn homing enemies with health that die when shot.
  5. Add a crosshair, health HUD, and mouse release on pause.

title: 3D Physics, AnimationTree & a Third-Person Shooter
html:
A new genre: over the shoulder

This lesson starts the third-person shooter genre as a real 3D game, building on your FPS knowledge. The big new pieces are a SpringArm3D camera that follows the player and avoids clipping through walls, a fully blended 3D character AnimationTree, and aiming where the camera and character orientation differ.

The SpringArm camera rig

A SpringArm3D is a node that casts a ray backward and pulls its child camera in when something is in the way — exactly the behavior you want over the shoulder. Build the rig as: player → Node3D (CameraPivot) → SpringArm3D → Camera3D. The pivot rotates from the mouse; the spring arm handles distance and collision.

camera_rig.gd
# extends Node3D (the CameraPivot) const SENS := 0.005 @onready var arm: SpringArm3D = $SpringArm3D func _ready() -> void: Input.mouse_mode = Input.MOUSE_MODE_CAPTURED arm.spring_length = 4.0 func _unhandled_input(e: InputEvent) -> void: if e is InputEventMouseMotion: rotate_y(-e.relative.x * SENS) rotation.x = clampf(rotation.x - e.relative.y * SENS, -1.2, 0.4)
Tip.
Set the SpringArm3D's collision mask to the world/environment layer only. If it collides with the player it will jam the camera inside the character.

Camera-relative movement

In a TPS the character moves relative to the camera, then turns to face the direction it moves. We read the camera's flattened basis to convert input into world movement, then slerp the model's rotation toward it.

tps_player.gd
# extends CharacterBody3D const SPEED := 5.5 @onready var pivot: Node3D = $CameraPivot @onready var model: Node3D = $Model func _physics_process(delta: float) -> void: if not is_on_floor(): velocity += get_gravity() * delta var input := Input.get_vector("left", "right", "up", "down") var basis := pivot.global_transform.basis var dir := (basis.x * input.x + basis.z * input.y) dir.y = 0 dir = dir.normalized() velocity.x = dir.x * SPEED velocity.z = dir.z * SPEED if dir.length() > 0.1: var target := atan2(dir.x, dir.z) model.rotation.y = lerp_angle(model.rotation.y, target, 12.0 * delta) move_and_slide() _update_anim(dir.length())

Blended 3D animation

Imported character models (glTF) bring their own animations into an AnimationPlayer. To blend idle/walk/run by speed, use an AnimationTree with a BlendSpace1D (or a 2D blend for strafing). Feed it the current speed each frame:

tps_player.gd (anim)
@onready var tree: AnimationTree = $AnimationTree func _update_anim(speed01: float) -> void: # BlendSpace1D from 0 (idle) to 1 (run) tree.set("parameters/Locomotion/blend_position", speed01) func _fire() -> void: # trigger a OneShot node layered over locomotion tree.set("parameters/Shoot/request", AnimationNodeOneShot.ONE_SHOT_REQUEST_FIRE)

A OneShot node plays a shoot animation on top of the locomotion blend, so the character can run and fire at once — the layered animation that defines third-person shooters.

Aiming from the camera in third person

The character faces movement, but bullets must go where the camera aims. Raycast from the camera's center through the world; the character's gun points at that hit point. This is the classic third-person aiming trick: cast from screen center, not from the muzzle.

tps_player.gd (aim)
@onready var cam: Camera3D = $CameraPivot/SpringArm3D/Camera3D func _aim_point() -> Vector3: var from := cam.global_position var to := from + (-cam.global_transform.basis.z) * 100.0 var q := PhysicsRayQueryParameters3D.create(from, to) var hit := get_world_3d().direct_space_state.intersect_ray(q) return hit.position if hit else to
Law.
In a third-person shooter, the camera aims and the body follows. Decouple where the character looks from where it moves, or aiming while strafing will feel wrong.
Lab.
Add an aim mode: on right-click, shorten the spring_length and pull the camera over the shoulder with a Tween, tightening accuracy. Releasing returns to the wide follow.
🎮 Build This: Third-Person Shooter (3D) — Cover Shooter

This starts the third-person shooter genre as a real 3D game, reusing 3D physics, raycasting, and enemies from the FPS lesson.

  1. Build a SpringArm3D camera rig that orbits with the mouse and avoids walls.
  2. Implement camera-relative CharacterBody3D movement that rotates the model toward motion via lerp_angle.
  3. Drive idle/walk/run with an AnimationTree BlendSpace1D and layer a shoot OneShot.
  4. Aim by raycasting from the camera center; fire bullets toward that point at enemies with health.
  5. Add an over-the-shoulder aim mode that tweens the spring length on right-click.

title: 3D Racing — Vehicle Physics, Cameras & Track Design
html:
A new genre: let physics drive

This lesson starts the racing genre as a real 3D game. Unlike the controllers you have written by hand, a car uses Godot's built-in VehicleBody3D, a specialized rigid body with raycast wheels that simulate suspension, traction, and slip. Your job shifts from writing motion code to tuning physics parameters and designing a track.

Building the car

A VehicleBody3D needs a mesh, a collision shape, and four VehicleWheel3D children positioned where the wheels go. Mark the front two as steering, the rear two (or all four) as driven. Wheels are themselves raycasts: they probe the ground for suspension, so they must hang slightly below the chassis.

PropertyEffect
massHeavier = more stable, slower to turn
suspension_stiffnessHigher = firmer, less body roll
wheel_friction_slipGrip; lower = drifty, higher = sticky
use_as_tractionThis wheel receives engine force
use_as_steeringThis wheel turns with steering input

Driving input

You set three values on the body each frame: engine_force (gas/reverse), brake, and steering (radians). Smooth the steering so the wheels do not snap.

car.gd
# extends VehicleBody3D const MAX_ENGINE := 220.0 const MAX_STEER := 0.5 const BRAKE_FORCE := 8.0 func _physics_process(delta: float) -> void: var accel := Input.get_axis("brake", "gas") engine_force = accel * MAX_ENGINE # brake when reversing intent while moving forward brake = BRAKE_FORCE if Input.is_action_pressed("handbrake") else 0.0 var steer_input := Input.get_axis("right", "left") steering = lerp(steering, steer_input * MAX_STEER, 6.0 * delta)
Tip.
Lower the car's center of mass to stop rollovers. Add a small invisible collision mass low in the chassis, or in code nudge the body so weight sits below the wheels. High, twitchy cars flip on the first corner.

The chase camera

A racing camera should lag slightly behind and look ahead — it follows position but smooths so the car feels fast. Use a separate Camera3D (not parented to the car) that interpolates toward a point behind the car each frame:

chase_cam.gd
# extends Camera3D @export var target: Node3D @export var back := 7.0 @export var up := 3.0 func _physics_process(delta: float) -> void: var t := target.global_transform var goal := t.origin + t.basis.z * back + Vector3.UP * up global_position = global_position.lerp(goal, 5.0 * delta) look_at(t.origin + Vector3.UP * 1.0, Vector3.UP)

Multiple cameras and viewports

Racing games love camera variety: chase, hood, and a rear-view mirror. Swap the active camera by calling make_current() on whichever you want. For a true mirror or split-screen, render a second view with a SubViewport holding its own camera and display it in a TextureRect — that is how picture-in-picture rear views are made.

The track and lap timing

Build a track from mesh segments with collision (a CSGPolygon3D extruded along a Path3D is a fast way to prototype a road). Lap timing uses ordered checkpoint Area3D nodes: the car must cross them in sequence, and crossing the finish after the last checkpoint records a lap.

lap_manager.gd
# extends Node var total_checkpoints := 4 var next_index := 0 var lap := 0 var lap_time := 0.0 var best := INF func _process(delta: float) -> void: lap_time += delta func checkpoint_crossed(index: int) -> void: if index != next_index: return # wrong order, ignore (no shortcuts) next_index += 1 if next_index >= total_checkpoints: next_index = 0 lap += 1 best = minf(best, lap_time) lap_time = 0.0

Each checkpoint Area3D connects body_entered to LapManager.checkpoint_crossed(my_index). The ordering check prevents the player from driving backward through the finish to cheese laps.

Watch out.
VehicleWheel3D nodes must be children of the VehicleBody3D and positioned at the wheel locations — not visually faked. If a car sinks into the ground or will not move, the wheels are misplaced or set to neither traction nor steering.
Lab.
Add a speedometer reading linear_velocity.length() * 3.6 (km/h) to a HUD, and a key that cycles chase → hood → far cameras via make_current().
🎮 Build This: Racing (3D) — Time-Trial Circuit

This starts the racing genre as a real 3D game in a new project, reusing your 3D scene/lighting setup from the FPS lesson.

  1. Build a VehicleBody3D car with four VehicleWheel3D wheels (front steering, rear traction) and a low center of mass.
  2. Wire gas/brake/steer input and tune mass, suspension, and friction until it feels good.
  3. Add a smoothed chase camera and a second camera you can toggle with make_current().
  4. Lay out a closed track with road collision and four ordered checkpoint Area3D nodes.
  5. Add a LapManager that times laps in order and shows current and best lap on a HUD.

title: Match-3 Puzzle — Grids, Tweens & Cascades
html:
A new genre: the grid is the game

This lesson starts the match-3 puzzle genre in a new 2D project. After a string of physics and action games this is a refreshing change: a match-3 is almost pure data. The board is a 2D array of integers; matching, falling, and refilling are operations on that array; and Tween animations make the data changes feel satisfying. Get the data model right and the rest follows.

The data-driven grid

Represent the board as a dictionary keyed by Vector2i cell, storing the gem node at each spot. Keeping cell logic in grid coordinates (not pixels) makes every algorithm clean; convert to pixels only when placing nodes.

board.gd
# extends Node2D const W := 8 const H := 8 const CELL := 64 const TYPES := 5 @export var gem_scene: PackedScene var grid: Dictionary = {} # Vector2i -> gem node func cell_to_px(c: Vector2i) -> Vector2: return Vector2(c.x, c.y) * CELL func _ready() -> void: _fill_board() func _fill_board() -> void: for y in H: for x in W: var c := Vector2i(x, y) _spawn_gem(c, _safe_type(c)) func _safe_type(c: Vector2i) -> int: # avoid creating an instant match on initial fill var t := randi() % TYPES while _would_match_at(c, t): t = randi() % TYPES return t

Swapping two gems

The player swaps two adjacent gems. Swap them in the data, check for matches; if none result, swap back — the failed-swap animation that tells the player the move was illegal.

board.gd (swap)
func try_swap(a: Vector2i, b: Vector2i) -> void: if a.distance_to(b) != 1.0: return _swap_data(a, b) await _animate_swap(a, b) var matches := _find_matches() if matches.is_empty(): _swap_data(a, b) # revert await _animate_swap(a, b) else: await _resolve(matches) func _swap_data(a: Vector2i, b: Vector2i) -> void: var ga: Node = grid[a] var gb: Node = grid[b] grid[a] = gb grid[b] = ga

Finding matches

Scan every cell for a run of three or more of the same type horizontally and vertically. Collect matched cells into a set so overlapping runs (an L or T shape) are not double-counted.

board.gd (matches)
func _find_matches() -> Array: var found := {} for y in H: for x in W: _scan_run(Vector2i(x, y), Vector2i(1, 0), found) _scan_run(Vector2i(x, y), Vector2i(0, 1), found) return found.keys() func _scan_run(start: Vector2i, dir: Vector2i, found: Dictionary) -> void: var t: int = grid[start].type var run := [start] var c := start + dir while grid.has(c) and grid[c].type == t: run.append(c) c += dir if run.size() >= 3: for m in run: found[m] = true

Resolve: clear, fall, refill, cascade

Resolving a match is a four-step loop that repeats while new matches keep forming — the cascade that makes match-3 feel alive:

  1. Clear matched gems (tween scale to zero, then free).
  2. Collapse: gems above empty cells fall down to fill gaps.
  3. Refill: drop new gems from the top into the remaining holes.
  4. Re-check: if the new arrangement has matches, repeat.
board.gd (resolve)
func _resolve(matches: Array) -> void: while not matches.is_empty(): Score.add(matches.size() * 10) for c in matches: var g: Node = grid[c] grid.erase(c) _pop(g) # tween + free await _collapse_columns() await _refill() matches = _find_matches() # cascade func _pop(g: Node) -> void: var t := create_tween() t.tween_property(g, "scale", Vector2.ZERO, 0.12) t.tween_callback(g.queue_free)

Falling with tweens

When a column collapses, move each surviving gem to its new cell and tween its position so it appears to drop. New gems spawn one row above the top and tween in, so refills look like gravity.

board.gd (fall tween)
func _move_gem(g: Node, to: Vector2i) -> void: grid[to] = g var t := create_tween() t.tween_property(g, "position", cell_to_px(to), 0.18) \ .set_trans(Tween.TRANS_QUAD).set_ease(Tween.EASE_IN)
Tip.
await a tween's finished signal (or the longest one) before scanning for the next cascade, so animations and logic stay in lockstep. Resolving the data before the gems finish moving causes flicker and ghost matches.
Law.
Separate state from presentation. The grid dictionary is the truth; tweens are just how the player sees it change. You could run the entire game with no graphics and the logic would still be correct.
Lab.
Add a move counter and a target score for a win condition, plus a deadlock check: if no swap would create a match, shuffle the board.
🎮 Build This: Match-3 Puzzle — Gem Swap

This starts the match-3 puzzle genre in a new 2D project, reusing the signal and autoload patterns (a Score singleton) from earlier arcade lessons.

  1. Build an 8x8 board stored as a Vector2i-keyed dictionary, filled with no initial matches.
  2. Let the player click-drag to swap adjacent gems, reverting illegal swaps with a tween.
  3. Implement horizontal and vertical match detection collecting cells into a set.
  4. Resolve matches with clear → collapse → refill → re-check cascades, each step tweened.
  5. Add a Score autoload, a HUD, a move limit, and a board reshuffle when no moves remain.
